records.content:
During this project, we first explored several datasets as a group and selected those related to education and lifestyle. These datasets allowed us to examine how different lifestyle factors may influence educational performance. Based on this, we aimed to answer the question of whether a strict lifestyle or a more balanced lifestyle leads to better academic outcomes. To investigate both perspectives, we created eight visualizations for each side, presenting evidence that either supports or challenges the effectiveness of each lifestyle.

Our findings suggest that a stricter lifestyle is generally associated with better educational performance. However, the data also indicates that this approach may lead to higher levels of stress. This highlights a trade-off between academic success and well-being, suggesting that while strict routines can improve performance, they may come at a personal cost.

When choosing the topic, our team decided to collect four datasets from Kaggle to discover how different factors influence students’ academic performance. Although each dataset contains different variables and was collected from different populations, they all focus on the relationship between students’ study time and their academic performance. Instead of merging dataset, we used each dataset to answer specific substantiation within our perspectives. Based on the datasets, we developed two different perspectives to compare how different student lifestyles (strict versus chill lifestyles) impact on academic performance.

Dataset 1: Student lifestyle and academic performance (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/steve1215rogg​/student​-lifestyle​-dataset)

This dataset provides information about different factors in student lifestyle and their academic performance (represented by GPA) and student well-being. This dataset contains 2000 records and 8 variables.

Dataset 2: Gaming dataset (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/aiexplorer77​/gaming​-vs​-academic​-performance)

This dataset has 8000 records and 14 variables and has information on many different things that have to do with gaming and education.

Dataset 3: Alcohol consumption dataset (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/uciml​/student​-alcohol​-consumption).

This dataset consists of 33 columns and 393 rows, providing information on a variety of aspects like demographics and alcohol consumption.

Dataset 4: Student Habits vs Academic Performance (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/jayaantanaath​/student​-habits​-vs​-academic​-performance​/data)

This dataset contains habit-related variables such as sleep hours and diet quality. Overall, this dataset has 1000 cases in total and 16 variables.

Variables used in the data story are listed and categorized below.
Dataset 1 (D1) - Student lifestyle and academic performance:

Stress level (ordinal): Level of stress reported in 3 levels - low, moderate, high

GPA (ratio): Average grade on a 4.0 max scale

Dataset 2 (D2) - Gaming vs Academic Performance:

Gaming hours (ratio): Average gaming hours per day

Reaction time (ratio): Average response time

Grades (ratio): Final grade score from 0-100 scale

Grade category (nominal): classifies students’ performance into three ordered levels: fail, sufficient, and good.

Dataset 3 (D3) - Student Alcohol Consumption:

Study time (ordinal): Weekly study time in four categories (1 = <2 hours, 2 = 2-5 hours, 3 = 5-10 hours, 4 = > 10 hours)

Final grade (ratio): Final grade (G3) on 0-20 scale

Weekday alcohol consumption (ordinal): Alcohol consumption during weekdays rated from very low (1) to very high (5)

Weekend alcohol consumption (ordinal): Alcohol consumption during weekends rated from very low (1) to very high (5)

Absences (ratio): Number of school absences

Paid extra classes (nominal): Whether the student attended paid extra educational support (Yes/No).

Dataset 4 (D4) - Student Habits vs Academic Performance:

Study hours per day (ratio): Average daily study time.

Exam score (ratio): Final examination score.

To preprocess datasets above, we first inspect the descriptive details of each dataset and remove irrelevant variables. Then, we checked for missing values and duplicate records which we removed. To handle missing values, we used median imputation for numerical variables and mode imputation for categorical variables. Besides, we also check for outliers. Lastly, we removed illogical datapoints, for example those who have grades above 100 (the maximum of grading scale used) in dataset 3.

Specific preprocessing attempts in perspective 1

In dataset 1, we encoded students’ grade variables into categorical variables called Grade Group using percentile tertile grouping (pandas.qcut), which divides students’ grade into three approximately equal-sized groups: Low, Medium, High.
2. Specific preprocessing attempts in perspective 2

In dataset 3, we generalised the grades from 1-20 to 1-10. Also, we decided to make a new column called ‘Alcohol’ to generalise alcohol consumption over the entire week by adding the columns workday alcohol (Walc) and weekend alcohol (Dalc) together and dividing the new number by two, so we remain with a 1 to 5 scale.

\text{Normalized Study Time}
=
\frac{x-x_{\min}}{x_{\max}-x_{\min}}\times10

The x is the original study time,  x_{\min}  and  x_{\max}  are the minimum and maximum study time values within each dataset. This allows the comparison between datasets in study time to become meaningful and relative.
For example: In Dataset 4, study time ranges from 0 to 8.3 study hours per day. A student who studies 6 hours is rescaled as:\frac{6.0 - 0}{8.3 - 0} \times 10 = 7.23

Then, a study time of 6 is represented as 7.23 on the 0-10 scale.

What factors help improve academic performance has always been a crucial question of generations of students and also researchers in the education field (e.g. 

Adelantado-Renau et al. (2019), 

Singh et al. (2018), 

Camacho-Morles et al. (2021)).

In some perspectives, a successful study method is investing most of study time per day without any distraction from social activities, gaming or entertainment. Previous study shows that study habits, skills, and attitudes can predict student academic performance, meaning that having serious learning behaviors for everyday plays an important role in grades (

Credé & Kuncel (2008)).

However, nowadays, student life is not just studying. They also spent time socializing and participating in other physical or extracurricular activities. Studying in a strict way might not be the most effective method to acquire high scores. 

Barbosa et al. (2020) found that sports participation may benefit students’ academic achievement. Besides, according to 

Gilman et al. (2003) findings, participating in extracurricular activities in a structured way can also enhance school performance of students.

Therefore, this data story aims to investigate whether living chill or living strictly would be more beneficial to students’ study, and the role of external variables such as stress level and parental supervision in the relationship between type of lifestyle and academic performance. Four datasets shared on Kaggle were used to structure the story: “Student lifestyle dataset” (

Gilman et al. (2003)


https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/steve1215rogg​/student​-lifestyle​-dataset), “Gaming vs Academic Performance” (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/aiexplorer77​/gaming​-vs​-academic​-performance), “Student Alcohol Consumption” (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/uciml​/student​-alcohol​-consumption) and “Student Habits vs Academic Performance” (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/jayaantanaath​/student​-habits​-vs​-academic​-performance).
Having a strict lifestyle, such as focusing more on studying and reducing leisure activities, may improve students’ academic performance.

Students spending more time studying tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run ../plots/study_time_plot.ipynb



Figure 1. Average Study Time by Grade Group Across Four Datasets

The study time was chosen as the first and important indicator of strict lifestyle because it shows the investment and priority of students in their academic achievement by allocating more time studying. The figure indicates that students who invest more study time tend to achieve higher grades. Across four datasets, the high grade group shows the highest average study time, while the low grade group consistently spends the least one. Additionally, the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset reveals a small and consistent difference between subjects. Students studying Mathematics report slightly more study time than those studying Portuguese. This is due to different learning demands of two subjects, as mathematics often requires more structured problem-solving tactics, while language relies heavily on continuous exposure, self-practicing and application in reality (

Foster (2023), 

Zhang & Zou (2020)).

Although higher study time is correlated with higher academic performance, showing across four datasets, study time alone does not fully explain students’ grades. It can still depend on learning strategies, quality, attention, and motivation of students (

Rosário et al. (2012)). Therefore, study time can be described as one of the important indicators of academic investment rather than a sole determinant.

Students with fewer absences tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run ../plots/attendance_plot.ipynb



Figure 2. Average Absences by Grade Group in Mathematics and Portuguese

The figure shows a clear relationship between study discipline and academic performance when measuring how many days students’ absences in the class. In both Mathematics and Portuguese, students in high grade group show fewest average absences, while medium and low grade groups are absent more frequently. 

Credé et al. (2010) investigate that absenteeism can lead to lower grades, as missing classes reduces learning opportunities and classroom engagement. Moreover, mathematics students report more absences than Portuguese students across all grade groups, indicating slight differences in academic discipline between two subjects.

Students who get higher grades tend to combine intensive study time with extra classes

%run ../plots/extra_class_plot.ipynb



Figure 3. Average Final Grade by Study Time and Paid Extra Classes

The visualization indicates that the relationship between extra classes and study time, and average of final grade differs between mathematics and Portuguese. The patterns are not fully linear, the trend fluctuated at some points. For Mathematics, students who did not attend extra classes experienced a slight decline in grades when studying 1 to 2 hours, followed by reaching a peak at 3 hours before dropping again at 4 hours. In contrast, students attending extra classes show a stable upward trend with increased average grades. This is because whether math students attend extra classes or spend more time studying may not fully explain their grades; their performance may also depend on math-solving tactics, learning strategies, and conceptual understanding ( 

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024), 

TURGUT (2021)). In terms of Portuguese, students who spend more time studying without extra classes tend to achieve higher grades than those who combine both of them. Interestingly, the group attending extra classes displayed a fluctuating pattern, with higher grades when studying 2 hours, but falling noticeably at 3 hours and recovering slightly at 4. This difference lies in the characteristics of the subject: mathematics requires problem-solving methods and teaching techniques from teachers, as well as concentration and comprehension from students. (

Alegre-Ansuategui et al. (2017),  

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024)), while with foreign languages, self-study and practice in reality will be more effective in improving students’ language skills and vocabulary ( 

Nakata (2016), 

Zhang & Zou (2022)).

Despite may affect mental wellbeing, strictly follow intense study schedule brings fruitful result

%run ../plots/pers1_subarg4.ipynb





Figure 4 Grade Distribution by Stress Level

In fiIt is quite clearly shown that the interquartile range of the grades of students in the high stress group spread around the highest interval and the median is accordingly higher. This supports that students with high stress typically score higher than those with moderate and low stress on average. This phenomenon can be explained by the concept of eustress, which is a type of positive stress that result in positive outcome, which stems from the Yerkes-Dodson Law, which states that ‘‘increasing stress is beneficial to performance until some optimum level is reached…’’ (

Le Fevre et al. (2003))
However, it is worth noting that grades spread quite widely in the high stress group, which is shown more clearly in the with-datapoints version, thus the pattern may not apply universally all the time.
Overall, data shows that high stress correlates with high grades. Since strict, intensive study schedules are a very plausible reason students report high stress (rather than e.g. financial stress or personal issues, given this is a student lifestyle dataset), this chart visually supports the idea that strict lifestyles pay off academically.
The balanced lifestyle is about combining studying and leisure activities. In this part we will show how alcohol consumption and gaming impact education performance.

%run ../plots/alcohol_consumption_plot1.ipynb



The datasets student-math and student_portuguese, both part of the alcohol consumption dataset, show a downward trend in the intensity of drinking, shown on the x-axis, and the percentage of students who participates in drinking that often, shown on the y-axis. A high percentage of the students don’t drink at all (1 on the x-axis) and a very small percentage drinks everyday or almost every day (4.5-5 on the x-axis). This graph gives a clear illustration of the distribution of drinking intensity among students in these datasets. this bar-chart of the datasets also shows that there is no clear correlation between the course, math or Portuguese, and the percentage of students who participate at drinking at a certain intensity.

%run ../plots/alcohol_consumption_plot.ipynb



The datasets student-math and student_portuguese, also show a very subtle trend when it comes to drinking intensity, here also shown on the x-axis, and the final grade at either math or Portuguese, shown on the y-axis. The medians of the final grade related to the alcohol intensity tend to drop slightly when the intensity increases. This is mostly the case at the Portuguese dataset, while we see the median drop from a 6.5 to a 5.0 when the drinking intensity increases from 1 to 5. At the math dataset on the other hand, we see a slight decline of the median from a 5.5 to a 4.0 when the intensity increases from 1 to 3, but after the median increases again to a 5.5 at intensity 5. This can be due to lack of observations for the 4 and 5 intensity in the student math dataset or it can be due to a less powerfull impact on grades when we consider a higher alcohol intensity and maths.

The second argument that we bring for the balanced lifestyle perspective is that spending more time gaming will reduce grades, but may improve response times which in turn will improve eduaction performance. This is based on the research of 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020), who found that higher response times improve cognitive performance. 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020) proved that gaming improves response times. This means that gaming would improve

%run ../plots/gaming_stats_3d.ipynb



%run ../plots/gaming_response_time.ipynb





The two graphs suggest that reaction time does not appear to have a strong influence on grades, because the data points do not show a clear or consistent pattern. In contrast, the amount of gaming seems to be more strongly related to grades, since the trend in that graph is more noticeable. Taken together, this means our results do not support the idea that reaction time is an important predictor of grades in this dataset. That is interesting because it seems to contradict 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020), who reports a different relationship between these factors and academic performance. The paper suggests that reaction time has a positive impact on educational performance because faster reaction times may reflect quicker information processing, better attentional control, or more efficient cognitive functioning, which can support learning and test performance.

We used bar charts to compare the average study time across low, medium, and high grade groups. We used four datasets to show consistent patterns of the data that high grade groups show more investment on their study time, not to compare between datasets from different schools. To ensure the meaningful comparability across datasets, study time was rescaled into the same 0-10 scale, clearly showing how students’ study time within the same time frame affected their scores. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, the dataset was appended into a single table, then created new column called “subject” to distinguish different subjects that students joined. The legend was assigned using subject variable to show the distinct comparison between mathematics and Portuguese students’ study time affecting their grades. This approach allows us to indicate the consistent pattern across datasets while avoiding misleading comparison between different grading systems and measurement scales.

For this substantiation, we used grouped bar charts to compare the average absent number of Mathematics and Portuguese students across grade groups. The x-axis indicates the students’ grade group, while y-axis shows the average number of absences. The legend was used to encode two subjects, helping audience distinguish whether the absence pattern differs between two subjects. This figure clearly highlights the strong relationship between academic discipline and their achievement.

In this substantiation, the line chart was used to illustrate the relationship between combined study time and average final grade differs depending on whether students attended extra classes or not. For both charts, the final grade is represented as the y-axis due to it is a continuous variable, while study time is shown on the x-axis as it is an ordinal variable. The two colored lines show students who attended extra classes and those who did not attend, coding as the legend of the graph. This design is useful because it shows the difference between extra-class groups, and the patterns change across different levels of study time.

We believe median is the most suitable statistic to represent the typical performance of the group. This is because the size of different stress groups are not equal. High stress has the most students, thus extreme values are more likely to occur by chance in this group, dragging the mean around. From that, boxplot is chosen as the graph that not only displays the median effectively but also shows the distribution of students’ grades in each group. Two versions of graphs (with and without datapoints) are available for readers who want to inspect the spread of outliers in detail. Contrast of colors is utilized: darker shade of red delivers an increasing level of stress.

The primary mark used in this visualization is area, this is represented by rectangular bars. This mark was chosen because bar-charts are highly effective at showing proportions and frequencies due to it being represented by the length and area of each bar on the chart. We didn’t use point or line marks because these are mostly relevant for visualisations that represent single observations or continuous trends which isn’t the case for us.

As visual channel we mainly used position for the x- and y-axis which range from 1 to 5 and 0 to 50 percent respectively. This is done because numerical values from 1 to 5 are very easy to interpret for users and percentages are great at representing quantitative comparison. in addition, we also used the channels size and color. Size is encoded by the height of the bar that can be seen on the visualization and color is encoded by giving both datasets (math and Portuguese) a different color to make them easily distinguishable for the user. lastly, we didn’t use other channels such as value, texture, orientation, and shape because they would only over complexify the visualization and wouldn’t add additional meaningfulness for the user.

The visualization contains several data types, it primarily consists of ordinal data, this is because the alcohol consumption is measured in levels that can be ordered from 1 to 5 to represent ordered categories ranging from low to high alcohol consumption. This visualization also uses nominal and ratio data, the nominal data is represented by the labels math and Portuguese in de visualization in addition of different colors, the ratio data can be found on the y-axis where we used percentages to represent the bar length. Percentages are ratio data, because they have a meaningful zero point and allow comparisons.

The visualizations were designed to show the relationship between grades, reaction time, and gaming hours as clearly as possible. In the first figure, a 3D scatter plot is used so that all three variables can be displayed simultaneously: grades on one axis, reaction time on another, and gaming hours on the third. The axes are explicitly labeled, including the note that lower reaction times mean faster responses. Data points are colored by grade category using an intuitive traffic‑light palette (green for good, orange for sufficient, red for fail), which makes differences in performance easy to recognize at a glance and also keeps the categories visually consistent across figures. The amount of data points is reduced to improve visibility by taking a sample of 500 of the rows. This keeps hte same results, but make the graph more legible.

The second figure complements this view by combining several layers in a 2D layout. The central scatter plot shows grades against reaction time, with point size encoding gaming hours so that the third variable is still visible without requiring 3D rotation. Above this scatter, violin plots summarize the distribution of gaming hours for the different grade categories, making it easier to compare typical gaming behaviour between high‑ and low‑performing students rather than focusing on individual outliers. On the right, boxplots of grades by category display medians and variability, reinforcing the separation between good, sufficient, and failing groups. The same color scheme as in the first plot is reused so that the legend only needs to be learned once and the eye can move between figures without confusion.

On June 23th 2026, our group 5B had a short presentation with the class to pitch about the progress of the data storyline project and received some peer feedback from other teams.
The feedback mainly focused on strengthening the interpretation of our visualizations and showing the distinct comparisons across datasets in each substantiation clearer and more consistent. Below is the list of the feedback:

Normalize the study time into the same scale to make clearer comparisons across the dataset in figure 1.

Revise the study discipline substantiation by only focusing on the more distinct differences shown by the dataset 3 with absence level, as attendance percentages in other datasets show similarity across grade groups.

Clarify the justification for stress level in the strict lifestyle by emphasizing the impact of stress level on academic performance.

Simplify the alcohol consumption intensity by reducing the repeated patterns in the bar chart.

Remove the boxplot from grades versus reaction time with a gaming scatter plot.

Following the feedback, our team reviewed the suggestion carefully and agreed with all of the proper feedback. Then we started making changes to improve the data storyline project right after the session.
